Meet Fudge: a design reference engine for AI agents. Instead of asking AI to make another “modern, premium” interface from scratch, let it search nearly 10,000 real websites by fonts, colors, components, layouts, page types, and visual similarity. Fudge combines measured design evidence with screenshots, runs locally through MCP, and remembers references you save with its Chrome extension. Give your coding agent better taste and not more adjectives.

results.title: WebMCP explained: How browser agents can call web tools without scraping the DOM
results.content:
For consumer products and new experiences built around AI, MCP Apps make sense — you design the interaction surface for the agent from the ground up. For existing web applications, especially complex B2B tools with established UIs and user sessions, WebMCP is the lower-friction path. You're not redesigning the product for AI. You're extending what you already have.

The comparison that keeps coming up is responsive design. When mobile arrived, most teams didn't rebuild their web apps from scratch — they added responsive breakpoints and the site was mobile-ready. WebMCP offers a similar incremental path to agent-readiness: annotate your forms, register your key operations, and your web application can now be operated by an AI without re-architecting anything.

## Where WebMCP Is Headed

results.title: WebMCP: Turning web pages into tools for AI agents
results.content:
At its core, WebMCP is a way for web applications to expose functionality in frontend JavaScript via MCP-like tools, so that agents can have understandable, schema-based, predictable interfaces to interact with a web app, while preserving the human interactive elements. It aims to fill a gap between existing MCP servers, which are backend-only, and frontend web automation (Chrome DevTools, Playwright, etc.) that controls a browser via a human-based UI. For a good introduction into when and why to reach for WebMCP, we suggest the Chrome team’s excellent post “When to use WebMCP and MCP.” [...] Much work has focused on connecting backend systems to AI agents via protocols such as A2A (Agent-to-Agent), MCP (Model Context Protocol), and ACP (Agent Communication Protocol). Now the focus is turning to web applications: unlocking the built-in authentication, personalization, and user functionality that already lives in these apps. A good website user experience is evaporating as a digital moat; we’ll soon see a good agent experience as the critical need and differentiator. The challenge is finding the easiest way to bring this existing power to agents. [...] The setup here is really useful: just configure `@mcp-b/webmcp-local-relay` once in Claude Desktop, then open any number of WebMCP-enabled web applications in a local browser and call their respective tools. This reduces the number of separate MCP tool configurations needed and addresses concerns such as authentication and personalization by using whatever an existing web application already uses and is willing to expose via WebMCP tools.

At this point, we’ve seen WebMCP tools called from the browser console and from Claude Desktop, both of which rely on external AI. But what if the AI itself also ran in the browser? Let’s push this web AI thing a bit further and build a full multi-agent system where all the functionality lives on-device.
